explicit_text: I was born in Natick, Massachusetts on May 10th, 1947, but I didn't stay there very long. It was just the place of my birth before my family moved elsewhere. Natick marks the beginning of my life story, even though my upbringing happened in other places.

explicit_text: My mother, Mary Elizabeth Duffy, was born and raised in Wellesley, Massachusetts. She came from a warm and affectionate Irish background, which shaped much of my early family life. Wellesley represented a more refined community compared to where my dad grew up.

explicit_text: I attended Boston College after graduating high school in 1965, majoring in East European history. It was a family school—my father and uncles went there—and I wanted to follow in their footsteps. Although I didn't apply myself fully, Boston College was a good institution that provided me with a solid education.

explicit_text: My father was the executive director of Catholic Charities in Newburgh and Albany, New York. His career in social work deeply influenced my own path. Catholic Charities was part of the Catholic community that shaped my upbringing and family values.

explicit_text: I attended Saint Patrick's High School, a small Catholic school, for three of my four high school years. It was an all-boys school run by the Christian Brothers, known for their strict discipline. I left after three years because my mother didn't like the physical discipline used there.

explicit_text: After graduating from Boston College, I volunteered for Vista, which was like a domestic Peace Corps, for about a year and two months in Central Virginia. I worked with the National Welfare Rights Organization and helped organize welfare recipients. It was a maturing experience and one of the best of my life.

explicit_text: During my time as a Vista volunteer in Central Virginia, I worked with the National Welfare Rights Organization to help welfare recipients gain access to benefits. explicit_text: After my Vista service, I went to graduate school at Rutgers University and earned a master's degree in social work. Both my parents were social workers, so it was a natural path for me. It was also where I met my future wife, Faith Santangelo, during a group dynamics class.

explicit_text: I met Faith Santangelo two months into graduate school at Rutgers in a group dynamics class when we struck up a conversation in an elevator. explicit_text: About years ago, Faith was diagnosed with stage 4 lymphoma that affected 40% of her bone marrow. It was a very serious diagnosis, and we feared losing her. We sought treatment at MD Anderson Cancer Center where a relatively new protocol was available, which gave us hope.
